perturbation.original_text: (g) Governing Law; Choice of Venue. (i) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed within that state, without giving effect to any law or principals of law that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction. (ii) Each Party (A) consents to submit itself to the personal jurisdiction of the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware in the event any dispute arises out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, (B) agrees that it shall not attempt to deny or defeat such personal jurisdiction by motion or other request for leave from any such court, (C) agrees that it shall not bring any action relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement in any court other than the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware, and each Party irrevocably waives the right to trial by jury and (D) irrevocably consents to service of process by a reputable overnight mail delivery service, signature requested, to the address of such Party's principal place of business or as otherwise provided by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Governing Law; Choice of Venue. (i) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the general principles of law, potentially including the laws of the State of Delaware as deemed appropriate by the presiding court. (ii) Each Party (A) consents to submit itself to the jurisdiction of a court as determined at the sole discretion of the party initiating the legal proceedings, considering the nature of the dispute, (B) agrees that it will consider not denying or defeating such jurisdiction, (C) agrees that it will attempt to bring any action relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement in a court within a reasonable geographic proximity to its principal place of business, and each Party consents to service of process by mail, if convenient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Delaware law and Delaware courts for dispute resolution, ensuring predictability. The modified clause introduces ambiguity by suggesting 'general principles of law' and 'Delaware law as deemed appropriate,' undermining the certainty of Delaware law's application. The clause regarding jurisdiction is weakened by making it discretionary ('determined at the sole discretion of the party initiating the legal proceedings') and replacing 'shall not attempt to deny' with 'will consider not denying'. Furthermore, the venue clause is weakened by 'attempt to bring' and 'reasonable geographic proximity.' Finally, the service of process now becomes 'by mail, if convenient'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause mandates Delaware jurisdiction and venue, whereas the modified clause introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with 28 U.S.C. § 1404, which allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought, considering the convenience of parties and witnesses, and the interest of justice. The modification makes the venue uncertain and subject to the initiator's discretion, limiting the court's power for a change of venue, and also making service of process uncertain, thereby contradicting federal law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding jurisdiction and venue, potentially conflicting with 28 U.S.C. § 1404. The original clause mandates Delaware jurisdiction and venue, providing certainty. The modified clause introduces terms like 'general principles of law' and allows the party initiating legal proceedings to determine the court. It also changes 'shall not attempt to deny' to 'will consider not denying', weakening the jurisdiction agreement. Furthermore, it weakens the venue clause by changing it to 'attempt to bring' in a court within a 'reasonable geographic proximity'. These changes create ambiguities regarding the enforcement of the agreement, undermining the legal obligation established in the original contract. According to Scraped Law Snippet 2, 28 U.S.C. § 1404 (a), a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought for the convenience of parties and witnesses and the interest of justice. The changed contract text allows a party to choose a jurisdiction, which might impede the court's ability to transfer the case based on convenience and justice, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (h) Reimbursement. The Company shall reimburse the Marathon Parties for their reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, including the reasonable fees and expenses of counsel, incurred in connection with their engagement with the Company, the Nominations and the negotiation of, and all matters arising under this Agreement; provided, however, that such reimbursement shall not exceed $250,000.
perturbation.changed_text: (h) Reimbursement. The Company may, at its discretion, reimburse the Marathon Parties for expenses, if any, deemed reasonable by the Company's internal auditors, up to a possible maximum of $250,000, subject to budget availability and further review by the Company's CFO.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause creates a clear obligation for the Company to reimburse Marathon Parties' expenses up to $250,000. The modified clause introduces significant ambiguity and discretion. Replacing 'shall reimburse' with 'may, at its discretion, reimburse' makes the reimbursement entirely optional. Adding 'if any, deemed reasonable by the Company's internal auditors' introduces a subjective standard controlled by the Company. 'Possible maximum' weakens the cap, and the phrases 'subject to budget availability and further review by the Company's CFO' create additional conditions and uncertainties, undermining the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification conflicts with Delaware General Corporation Law § 141(a), which vests the management of the corporation in the board of directors. By making reimbursement discretionary and subject to internal auditor and CFO approval, the clause potentially infringes upon the Board's authority to manage the corporation's affairs, including financial obligations. While § 141(a) allows for agreements that restrict the board's power, the ambiguity introduced here undermines the agreement itself and could be interpreted as an unlawful delegation or restriction of the Board's management powers. Specifically, if the Board approved this agreement with the intention of providing certain reimbursement, the internal auditors and CFO approval process could frustrate the Board's initial intent, effectively circumventing their decision-making authority.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original obligation for reimbursement. The original text states "The Company shall reimburse," creating a mandatory obligation. The modified text replaces this with "The Company may, at its discretion, reimburse," which makes the reimbursement entirely optional. This shift undermines the initial agreement and introduces uncertainty. While the Law Explanation suggests a conflict with Delaware General Corporation Law § 141(a) related to board authority, the direct contradiction lies in altering a mandatory reimbursement to a discretionary one. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses the management of the corporation by the board, but the core issue here is the change from a mandatory to a discretionary obligation.
